section.title: God Is Our Refuge and Strength
section.sectionRationale: A song of Zion's security amid cosmic and political upheaval, this psalm's opening confession, 'God is our refuge and strength,' and its recurring refrain, 'the Lord of Hosts is with us,' proclaim the very truth fulfilled in Christ, Immanuel, God with us.
section.thesis: Though the earth shakes and nations rage, God's own presence with His people, pictured as a river gladdening the city of God, is unshakable refuge and strength, calling every anxious heart to be still and know that He is God, a peace fulfilled in Christ, Immanuel.
section.frameNote: Three movements marked by Selah: God our refuge amid cosmic upheaval, verses 1-3; God's presence in the city as the nations rage, verses 4-7; and the call to see God's works and be still, knowing He is God, verses 8-11.

units.label: God is our refuge and strength
units.note: God our refuge amid cosmic upheaval

verses.text: God is our {{machaseh|refuge}} and strength, an ever-present help in times of trouble.
Therefore we will not fear, though the earth is transformed and the mountains are toppled into the depths of the seas,
though their waters roar and foam and the mountains quake in the surge. Selah

units.label: The Lord of Hosts is with us
units.note: God's presence in the city as the nations rage

verses.text: There is a river whose streams delight the city of God, the holy place where the Most High dwells.
God is within her; she will not be moved. God will help her when morning dawns.
Nations rage, kingdoms crumble; the earth melts when He lifts His voice.
The LORD of Hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress. Selah

units.label: Be still and know that I am God
units.note: the call to see God's works and be still

verses.text: Come, see the works of the LORD, who brings devastation upon the earth.

He makes wars to cease throughout the earth; He breaks the bow and shatters the spear; 
He burns the shields in the fire.

"Be {{raphah|still}} and know that I am God; I will be exalted among the nations, 
I will be exalted over the earth."
The LORD of Hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress. Selah

machaseh.gloss: Refuge, shelter, a place of safety from storm or danger. The psalm opens by naming God Himself, not any earthly fortress, as this refuge, ever present, not distant, in times of trouble.

raphah.gloss: To slacken, let go, cease striving. God's command 'be still' is not mere quiet but a call to release human effort and striving, to stop fighting and trust the LORD's own sovereign action over the nations.

basic.context: Sung by the sons of Korah, this psalm confesses God as refuge and strength even when the natural world seems to collapse, mountains toppling into the sea, and when the political world rages, nations warring and kingdoms crumbling. Its refrain, 'the LORD of Hosts is with us,' anchors confidence not in Zion's walls but in God's own presence.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm does not promise the absence of trouble, mountains genuinely shake and nations genuinely rage, but promises God's unshakeable presence within that trouble, culminating in His own command to cease striving and know that He is God.
basic.originalLanguages: God Himself is called machaseh, refuge, verse 1, not a distant helper but an ever-present shelter. His climactic command in verse 10 is raphah, be still, literally to let go or slacken one's grip, a call to cease human striving and trust God's sovereign action.
basic.typology: The refrain 'the LORD of Hosts is with us,' God with us, anticipates Christ, Immanuel, God with us, Matthew 1:23, while the river gladdening the city of God anticipates the river of the water of life flowing from God's throne in the new creation, Revelation 22:1-2.
basic.christ: The psalm's confidence that God is with His people culminates in Christ, whose very name Immanuel means God with us, Matthew 1:23, and who Himself commands wind and waves to 'be still,' Mark 4:39, the same divine authority this psalm attributes to God alone.
basic.god: God is shown as refuge, strength, and unshakeable presence, active even in apparent chaos, He who both permits the roaring waters and commands the raging nations to be still, ultimately promising to be exalted over all the earth.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 1:23 names Jesus Immanuel, God with us, the fulfillment of this psalm's central confession. Mark 4:35-41 shows Jesus commanding the storm to be still with the same divine authority this psalm ascribes to God. Revelation 22:1-2 pictures the river of life flowing from God's throne, echoing this psalm's river.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's confidence that God's presence is unshakeable refuge amid every upheaval, natural or political, finds its fulfillment in Christ, Immanuel, God with us, who commands the very storms this psalm describes and who will one day be exalted over all the earth in full and final peace.

context.literary: A song of Zion structured in three stanzas marked by Selah, moving from cosmic upheaval, verses 1-3, to political turmoil met by God's presence, verses 4-7, to a direct divine command to be still, verses 8-11, with an identical refrain closing the last two stanzas.
context.historical: Likely composed during a period of national threat, perhaps the Assyrian siege of Jerusalem under Hezekiah, 2 Kings 19, when God's sudden deliverance of the city confirmed His presence as Zion's true defense regardless of surrounding chaos.
context.canonical: A foundational text for biblical confidence in God's presence amid crisis, its language, 'God is our refuge and strength' and 'be still and know that I am God,' has become a touchstone of Christian devotion in every generation of trouble.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground the covenant community's confidence not in circumstances, which genuinely shake and rage, but in God's own unshakeable presence, and to call His people to cease striving and trust His sovereign action over all nations.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical deliverance of Zion from national threat; it prescribes as an abiding pattern trusting God as refuge amid any upheaval and obeying His call to be still rather than striving in anxious self-reliance.

debates.question: Does verse 9, 'He makes wars to cease throughout the earth,' describe a past historical deliverance or point forward to a final, complete peace?

views.label: The verse primarily celebrates God's past historical deliverances of Jerusalem from specific military threats, such as the failed Assyrian siege
views.case: The immediately surrounding verses describe concrete acts, breaking bows, shattering spears, burning shields, language fitting a specific historical battle's aftermath rather than a purely future scene.

views.label: The verse points forward typologically to the final, complete cessation of war in God's eschatological kingdom, as in Isaiah 2:4
views.case: The psalm's climax in verse 10 promises God will be exalted 'among the nations' and 'over the earth' in language exceeding any single historical deliverance, suggesting a trajectory toward final, universal peace.

debates.bearing: Both readings work together: the God who acted decisively in past deliverances is the same God pledged to bring final and complete peace, so the psalm's confidence in present trouble draws directly on the assurance of ultimate future rest.

study.originalLanguages: God is named machaseh, refuge, verse 1, an ever-present shelter rather than a distant helper. His climactic command, raphah, be still, verse 10, calls His people to release their own striving and rest in His sovereign, coming exaltation over all the nations and the whole earth.

typology.type: The LORD of Hosts with us, the God of Jacob our fortress, refrain verses 7, 11
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, Immanuel, God with us, Matthew 1:23, the ultimate and permanent assurance of God's abiding presence with His people.

typology.type: The river whose streams gladden the city of God, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: The river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb, Revelation 22:1-2, and the living water Christ Himself gives, John 4:14.

study.christ: This psalm's central confession, 'God is our refuge and strength' and 'the LORD of Hosts is with us,' finds its fulfillment in Christ, whose very name Immanuel means God with us, Matthew 1:23. The same authority this psalm attributes to God, commanding the earth and the raging waters, Jesus exercises directly when He rebukes the storm and commands it, 'be still,' Mark 4:39, using the identical call to peace this psalm gives.
study.god: God is refuge, strength, and unshakeable presence within His people even as the created order and the nations rage around them. He is active in both permitting upheaval and commanding it to cease, and promises His own final exaltation over all the earth.

crossReferences.note: Names Jesus Immanuel, God with us, the fulfillment of this psalm's central confession of God's presence.
Jesus commands the storm to be still with the same divine authority this psalm ascribes to God alone.
Pictures the river of the water of life flowing from God's throne, echoing this psalm's river gladdening the city of God.

study.oneStory: This psalm's confidence that God's presence is unshakeable refuge amid every upheaval finds its fulfillment in Christ, Immanuel, God with us, who commands the very storms this psalm describes and who will one day be exalted over all the earth in full and final peace.

sermon.title: Trusting God in Difficult Times, Psalm 46
sermon.why: In this meditation from his special series for difficult times, Keller works through Psalm 46 to show how naming God as refuge and strength, and hearing His command to be still, meets present fear and upheaval with settled confidence in God's presence.

quiz.q: What image portrays overwhelming trouble in verses 2-3?

options.text: Mountains toppling into the sea and roaring, foaming waters.
options.note: The psalm uses cosmic upheaval, the collapse of mountains and sea, as its picture of the worst possible trouble.

options.text: A gentle breeze disturbing calm waters.
options.note: The imagery is of violent, overwhelming upheaval, not a gentle disturbance.

options.text: A drought drying up the land.
options.note: The psalm's imagery is of overwhelming water and shaking mountains, not drought.

options.text: An army approaching from a distance.
options.note: Verses 2-3 use cosmic, natural imagery; armies and nations appear later, in verse 6.

quiz.q: What is present in the city of God, according to verse 4?

options.text: A river whose streams delight the city.
options.note: The river symbolizes God's own gladdening, life-giving presence within His city.

options.text: A towering fortress wall built by human hands.
options.note: The text names a river, not a human-built wall, as the source of gladness.

options.text: A great army stationed permanently at the gates.
options.note: The psalm's confidence rests in God's presence, symbolized by the river, not in military garrisons.

options.text: A marketplace filled with foreign traders.
options.note: The verse specifically names a river gladdening the city, not commercial activity.

quiz.q: What command does God give in verse 10?

options.text: "Be still and know that I am God."
options.note: This command calls people to cease striving and trust God's sovereign action over the nations and the earth.

options.text: "Take up arms and fight for your own defense."
options.note: The command is the opposite, to cease striving and let God act, not to take up arms.

options.text: "Flee to the mountains for safety."
options.note: The command calls for stillness and trust, not flight, in the face of danger.

options.text: "Build higher walls around the city."
options.note: The psalm's confidence rests in God's presence, not in human fortifications, and the command is to be still, not to build.

quiz.q: What title is repeated as a refrain in verses 7 and 11?

options.text: "The LORD of Hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress."
options.note: This refrain anchors the psalm's confidence in God's abiding presence and protection.

options.text: "The mountains will never be moved."
options.note: The psalm actually says the mountains can be toppled; the refrain is about God's presence with His people, not the mountains' stability.

options.text: "Our own strength will never fail."
options.note: The refrain credits God, not human strength, as the source of security.

options.text: "The nations will never rage again."
options.note: The psalm acknowledges the nations do rage, verse 6; the refrain's confidence is in God's presence despite that raging.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's confidence connect most directly to the New Testament?

options.text: Christ is Immanuel, God with us, the fulfillment of this psalm's central confession of God's presence.
options.note: Matthew 1:23 names Jesus Immanuel, directly fulfilling this psalm's refrain that God is with His people.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The psalm's language of God's presence and stilling power is directly picked up in the Gospels, especially Matthew 1:23 and Mark 4.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Christ's return.
options.note: The psalm speaks of God's presence and coming exaltation, not a specific date for any future event.

options.text: It is fulfilled only in the physical rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls.
options.note: The psalm's deepest fulfillment is in God's presence with His people in Christ, not merely in physical construction.

questions: The psalm names God as refuge and strength even while mountains topple and waters roar. What upheaval in your own life right now makes it hardest to believe God is truly your refuge?
God's presence, not Zion's walls, is what makes the city unshakeable, verses 4-5. Where are you tempted to trust in your own defenses rather than in God's presence with you?

God commands, 'Be still and know that I am God,' in the middle of raging nations and crumbling kingdoms. What would it look like this week to actually stop striving in a specific situation and trust God's sovereign action instead?

This psalm's refrain, 'the LORD of Hosts is with us,' is fulfilled in Christ, Immanuel, God with us. How does remembering that Jesus Himself is God with you change how you face today's specific troubles?
The psalm ends with God's promise to be exalted among the nations and over the earth. How does hoping in that final, universal peace shape how you respond to present conflict or fear?

translationNotes.note: Refuge renders machaseh, a place of shelter from storm or danger, here applied directly to God Himself rather than to any physical fortress.
The city of God identifies Jerusalem or Zion, though no literal river of this scale ran through the city, pointing beyond the literal terrain to God's own life-giving presence.
Be still renders raphah, to slacken or let go, a command to cease human striving and effort rather than merely to be quiet, addressed as much to warring nations as to anxious hearts.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 46:1-11. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Tim Keller's meditation 'Trusting God in Difficult Times, Psalm 46' at podcast.gospelinlife.com.
